title: Zagier's conjecture on zeta(F,4)
speaker: Alexander Goncharov
affiliation: Yale University; Member, School of Mathematics
abstract: Abstract: We prove that the weight 4 Beilinson's regulator map can be expressed via the classical n-logarithms, $n \leq 4$. This plus Borel's theorem implies Zagier's conjecture, relating the value of the Dedekind zeta functions at $s=4$ and the classical tetralogarithm. Another application is to the values of L-functions of elliptic curves over $Q$ at $s=4$. One of the new tools is a connection between cluster varieties and polylogarithms, generalising our work with V. Fock relating cluster varieties and the dilogarithm.

proof_stages.label: Classical regulator prototype and Zagier's higher-polylogarithmic formulation
proof_stages.summary: The talk starts from the class-number/regulator formula for the residue of a Dedekind zeta function, introduces Zagier's single-valued real polylogarithms L_n, and states the rough form of Zagier's conjecture as a determinant of L_n-values subject to algebraic conditions.

proof_stages.label: Bloch groups B_n and the polylogarithmic motivic complex
proof_stages.summary: For an arbitrary field F, the speaker defines the groups B_n(F) by quotienting the free group on F by relations R_n obtained from rational families lying in the kernel of the coproduct map δ_n, and assembles them into the complex B_n(F)→B_{n-1}(F)⊗F^*→⋯→Λ^nF^*.

proof_stages.label: Motivic and regulator meaning of the polylogarithmic complex
proof_stages.summary: The polylogarithmic complex is conjecturally identified with motivic cohomology/RHom(Q(0),Q(n)), hence with the Adams-graded pieces of algebraic K-theory, and its virtues are that it is a very small complex with explicit regulator maps to Deligne cohomology.

proof_stages.label: Weight-four theorem and deduction of Zagier's formula
proof_stages.summary: The main result is stated: in weight four there are canonical maps from K_{8-i}^{(4)}(F) to the cohomology of the polylogarithmic complex compatible with regulators, which for number fields gives elements in ker(δ_4) whose L_4-determinant computes ζ_F(4) up to the standard factors.

proof_stages.label: Mixed Tate motivic Lie coalgebra interpretation
proof_stages.summary: The speaker explains that the deeper meaning of the conjecture is a statement about the mixed Tate motivic Galois Lie coalgebra: its positive-weight part should be cofree, cogenerated by the groups B_n(F), a formulation stronger than standard Ext-group predictions.

proof_stages.label: Chevalley–Eilenberg bridge and the constructed weight-≤4 Lie coalgebra
proof_stages.summary: Using the Chevalley–Eilenberg cochain complex of a graded Lie coalgebra, the motivic prediction is reformulated, and the proof constructs a natural Lie coalgebra L_{≤4}(F) with L_1=F^*⊗Q, L_2≅B_2(F), L_3≅B_3(F), and an exact sequence B_4(F)→L_4(F)→Λ^2B_2(F).

proof_stages.label: Explicit weight-four functional equation for L_4
proof_stages.summary: The constructed L_4(F) is described concretely as a quotient encoding motivic versions of Li_4 and Li_{3,1}, with its key relation coming from a cyclic seven-point configuration on P^1, explaining why weight four is larger than B_4 alone and how the method may extend to higher weights.

examples.name: Dedekind zeta residue regulator formula
examples.note: The classical residue formula at s=1 is used as the motivating prototype that raises the question of replacing logarithms of units by higher polylogarithms in Zagier's conjecture.
examples.engagement_reason: He invokes the classical residue-at-s=1 formula and says the regulator is a determinant of logarithms, but does not derive or compute with it.

examples.name: Low-weight cases n=2 and n=3
examples.note: The known dilogarithm and trilogarithm cases, including the n=3 valuation condition, illustrate what the general Zagier conditions should look like before the talk turns to n=4.
examples.engagement_reason: He specializes Zagier’s conditions and the polylogarithmic complex to n=2 and n=3, spelling out the n=2 stopping point, the n=3 valuation condition, and the corresponding B2/B3 constructions.

examples.name: Bloch higher Chow cycle complexes as a contrast
examples.note: The speaker contrasts the polylogarithmic complex with Bloch's much larger cycle complexes to make concrete the claim that the proposed complexes are a smallest possible model for motivic complexes.

examples.name: The complex field case and the Borel regulator
examples.note: For F=C the theorem gives a map from K_7(C) to the kernel of B_4(C) to the real numbers, illustrating regulator compatibility in the most familiar Borel-regulator case.
examples.engagement_reason: He specializes the weight-four complex to F=C, traces K7(C) into the kernel of B4(C)→B3(C)⊗C*, then through L4 to R, and identifies the resulting map as the Borel regulator.

passes.reason: the complex-field case identifies the constructed regulator with the standard Borel regulator.
The complex-field specialization with the Borel regulator is a standard positive case of the regulator construction.

The F=C case and Borel regulator are used as a canonical special case of the theorem, not as a structurally exceptional object or failure case.
The complex-field case identifies the constructed regulator with Borel’s regulator rather than serving as a structurally anomalous example.

examples.name: Free absolute Galois group over the cyclotomic extension
examples.note: In response to a question about freeness, the conjectural freeness of the absolute Galois group over the cyclotomic extension of Q is cited as a number-theoretic analogue of the cofree Lie-coalgebra statement.
examples.engagement_reason: He states the conjectural pro-freeness of the absolute Galois group over the cyclotomic extension only as an analogy for freeness.

examples.name: Five-term and trilogarithm configuration relations
examples.note: The five-point P^1 relation for B_2 and the five-point P^2 relation for B_3 are recalled to show the low-weight pattern that the new weight-four relation generalizes.
examples.engagement_reason: He describes how the five-term relation is built from five points on P1 by forgetting points and taking cross-ratios, and similarly ties B3 relations to configurations of five points on P2.
